1: The "loud voice from the temple" commanding the seven angels to pour out the bowls echoes the command-voice structure of Exod 25-40 (God commanding Moses from the tabernacle) and the divine-command pattern of Ezek 9-10 (the divine voice commanding the judgment of Jerusalem). The bowls follow the pattern of the Exodus plagues (Exod 7-12) in a deliberate new-Exodus typology: the plagues on Egypt that forced Pharaoh's hand become the cosmic plagues on the new-Pharaoh (the beast) that precede the final deliverance. The whole creation now experiences the Exodus judgment that Egypt experienced -- with Rome as the new Egypt.
2: The first bowl -- "ugly, festering sores" on those who had the mark of the beast -- directly parallels the sixth plague on Egypt: "boils breaking out in sores on humans and animals throughout all the land of Egypt" (Exod 9:9-11). The parallel is exact in both the affliction and the selective targeting (those not in covenant with God are afflicted; Israel was protected in Goshen from several plagues). The Deuteronomic covenant curses add another layer: "The LORD will afflict you with... boils and with scurvy and with itch... and you cannot be healed" (Deut 28:27, 35) -- the bowl-plagues are the enacted Deuteronomic curses on a world that has chosen the beast over God.
3: The second bowl turning the sea to "blood like that of a dead person" -- killing every living creature -- parallels the first Egyptian plague (Exod 7:14-25 -- the Nile turned to blood, killing the fish and making the water undrinkable). The Revelation version is more total than the Exodus plague: all the sea's creatures die rather than just those in the Nile. The "blood like that of a dead person" (hos nekrou) -- coagulated, putrefied blood -- intensifies the horror beyond simple blood coloring; it is the blood of corpses, signaling that the sea is already a place of death.
4: The third bowl affecting rivers and springs parallels both the first Egyptian plague (the Nile, expanded here to all fresh water) and the third trumpet (8:10-11 -- a star poisoning the rivers and springs). The Lex Talionis logic is explicit in vv. 5-6: those who shed the blood of the saints receive blood to drink -- the punishment fits the crime in the most literal way. In ANE covenant curse formulas, the reversal of one's deeds upon oneself was standard judicial theology (Ps 9:15-16; Obad 15; Jer 50:15, 29).
5: The "angel of the waters" hymning God's justice introduces the angelology of elemental governance -- specific angels responsible for specific domains of creation, who themselves vindicate the justice of divine judgment. This tradition appears in 1 Enoch 69:22 (the angel of water), Jubilees 2:2 (angels over water), and the Dead Sea Scrolls (1QH 1:10-11). The angel's declaration "You are just (dikaios) in these judgments, you who are and who were, the Holy One" is a doxology in the face of divine judgment -- not grudging admission but worship through acknowledgment of God's judicial righteousness.
6: "For they poured out the blood of your saints and prophets, and you have given them blood to drink -- they deserve it" is the Lex Talionis applied at cosmic scale, echoing Isaiah's taunt against Babylon (Isa 49:26 -- "I will make your oppressors eat their own flesh, and they shall be drunk with their own blood as with wine") and Jer 51:35-36 (Babylon's violence against Jerusalem returned on her head). The martyrs' blood (6:9-11; 17:6; 18:24) is the cause; the blood-to-drink is the consequence. The formula "they deserve it" (axioi eisin) is standard in judicial pronouncements in apocalyptic -- the sentence fits the crime.
7: The altar "responding" with a doxology -- "Yes, Lord God Almighty, your judgments are true and just" -- anthropomorphizes the altar as the locus of the martyrs' prayers (6:9-11; 8:3-5) and the incense of intercession. The altar becomes the voice of answered prayer: the judgment is the answer to the cry "How long?" (6:10). The title "Lord God Almighty" (Kyrie ho theos ho pantokrator) is the LXX rendering of "LORD God of Hosts" -- the divine sovereign whose power is universal and whose judgments cannot be resisted.
8: The fourth bowl against the sun -- scorching people with fire -- echoes the prophetic imagery of Amos 8:9 ("I will make the sun go down at noon, and darken the earth in broad daylight") and Isa 30:26 (the sun becoming seven times brighter as eschatological light -- here reversed, the sun becomes judgment rather than blessing). The sun, which in the original creation order was given to "govern the day" (Gen 1:16), now scorches rather than sustains -- creation itself becomes the instrument of judgment against those who rejected the Creator.
9: The response of the victims -- "they blasphemed the name of God who had authority over these plagues and did not repent and give him glory" -- echoes the hardening of Pharaoh's heart (Exod 7:13, 22; 8:15, 19, 32; 9:7, 12, 34-35; 10:1, 20, 27; 11:10). Like Pharaoh, those who receive the plagues do not repent but harden in blasphemy. The refusal to repent (metanoia) is the defining mark of those who align with the beast -- they know the source of the plagues and actively blaspheme rather than turn. This is the NT application of Exod 9:16 (Pharaoh as the paradigm of the one who hardens himself against God's signs).
10: The fifth bowl poured on "the throne of the beast" -- plunging its kingdom into darkness -- parallels the ninth Egyptian plague (darkness over all Egypt for three days, Exod 10:21-23 -- "no one could see anyone else... but all the Israelites had light where they lived"). The beast's "throne" as the target signals that the center of the rival kingdom -- its political power-base -- is the specific locus of judgment. The darkness on the beast's kingdom is the deliberate inversion of the eschatological light promised to God's people (22:5 -- the Lord God is their light).
11: The second refusal to repent in the bowl sequence intensifies: "they cursed the God of heaven because of their pain and their sores." The "God of heaven" (ton theon tou ouranou) is a title used predominantly in Persian-period texts (Ezra 1:2; 6:9-10; 7:12; Neh 1:4; Dan 2:18-19, 37, 44) -- it acknowledges God's sovereign heavenly position while maintaining distance. The cursing of this God represents the final stage of the Pharaoh-pattern: recognizing the source of the plagues without any movement toward submission. The "sores" from the first bowl (v. 2) persist -- the plagues accumulate rather than pass.
12: The sixth bowl drying up the Euphrates River echoes God's drying up of the Red Sea for Israel's Exodus (Exod 14:21-22; Ps 106:9) and the Jordan (Josh 3:14-17), and Isa 11:15-16 (God drying the river to make a highway for the return of Israel from Assyria). Most specifically, it recalls the fall of Babylon to Cyrus (539 BCE): Herodotus and Xenophon report that Cyrus diverted the Euphrates to gain access to Babylon under its walls through the dry riverbed. The "kings from the east" evokes Cyrus (the king from the east who conquered Babylon) and the feared Parthian cavalry from beyond the Euphrates -- already a standing anxiety in Roman strategic planning throughout the first century.
13: The "three impure spirits like frogs" from the mouths of the unholy trinity (dragon, beast, false prophet) echoes the second Egyptian plague (frogs from the Nile covering all Egypt, Exod 8:1-15) and the demonic-spirit tradition of Second Temple Judaism (1 Enoch 19:1; Testament of Solomon 1-10). The frog-spirits as "demonic spirits performing signs" to gather kings recall the lying spirit that deceived the prophets of Israel into urging Ahab toward his death (1 Kgs 22:19-23) -- a heavenly-court scene in which God permits a deceptive spirit to operate, resulting in the death of the wicked king.
14: "Demonic spirits performing signs, going out to the kings of the whole world to assemble them for the battle on the great day of God the Almighty" frames the gathering of the nations as a divinely-permitted demonic operation. The "great day of God the Almighty" echoes Joel 2:11 ("the day of the LORD is great and very awesome") and Zeph 1:14-15 ("The great day of the LORD is near"). The nations assemble thinking they are preparing an offensive; they are actually being gathered for judgment -- the same pattern as Ezek 38-39 (Gog and Magog assembled from all nations against Israel, only to be destroyed by God's direct intervention).
15: "Look, I come like a thief" -- the parenthetical warning in 16:15 -- interrupts the narrative of demonic gathering with a pastoral word addressed directly to the churches. The "thief in the night" metaphor appears in Matt 24:43-44 (par. Luke 12:39-40), 1 Thess 5:2-4, and 2 Pet 3:10 -- the Lord's coming as unexpected, requiring constant readiness. "Blessed is the one who stays awake and keeps his clothes" echoes the exhortation to the Laodiceans (3:18 -- buy white garments) and the general call to watchfulness (Matt 25:1-13). The nakedness-shame connection (3:18; 16:15) draws on the Genesis 3 pattern: post-Fall nakedness as shame is the condition of those not clothed in Christ's righteousness.
16: Armageddon (Har Megiddon, "Mountain of Megiddo") is the only occurrence of this name in the Bible. Megiddo was a strategic fortress city controlling the Jezreel Valley -- the geographic corridor connecting Egypt and Mesopotamia -- and was the site of decisive ancient battles: Deborah and Barak's victory over Sisera (Judg 4-5) and Josiah's death in 609 BCE (2 Kgs 23:29-30; cf. Zech 12:11 -- "the mourning at Megiddo"). The "mountain" (har) of Megiddo is puzzling since Megiddo is in a valley; it may reference the adjacent Carmel range or represent the ideal symbolic battle-site. In any case, Megiddo had become a byword for decisive, world-determining battle -- the name is symbolic of the eschatological conflict.
17: The seventh bowl poured "into the air" -- with the accompanying voice from the throne saying "It is done!" (gegonen) -- echoes Jesus's final word from the cross (tetelestai, John 19:30) and God's declaration in 21:6 (gegona, "it is done"). The air as the domain of "the prince of the power of the air" (Eph 2:2 -- the Satan figure in Pauline thought) makes the seventh bowl a direct assault on Satan's domain. "It is done!" signals the completion of divine judgment. The theophanic response (lightning, thunder, earthquake) echoes the Sinai theophany (Exod 19:16-19; 20:18-21) -- God's direct self-revelation through natural convulsions.
18: The "severe earthquake -- no earthquake like it has ever occurred since mankind has been on earth" echoes the standard apocalyptic formula for the eschatological earthquake: Dan 12:1 ("a time of distress such as has not happened from the beginning of nations"), Matt 24:21 (great tribulation such as has not been from the beginning), and Ezek 38:19-20 (the earthquake of Gog's defeat that shakes the whole earth). The earthquake as divine judgment theophany pervades the OT (Isa 29:6; Amos 1:1; Zech 14:5; Ps 18:7-8). The unprecedented scale signals the finality of this judgment -- it cannot be followed by another because it is complete.
19: The "great city split into three parts" likely refers to Babylon/Rome (cf. chs. 17-18). The collapse of "the cities of the nations" signals the cosmic extent of this final judgment -- it is not one city but the whole network of human city-civilization built on the beast's authority. "God remembered great Babylon" -- the phrase "God remembered" is the covenantal memory language used of God's faithfulness to Noah (Gen 8:1), Abraham (Gen 19:29), Rachel (Gen 30:22), and Hannah (1 Sam 1:19); here it is memory that brings judgment rather than rescue.
20: "Every island fled away and the mountains could no longer be found" echoes the cosmic de-creation of 6:12-14 (the sixth seal -- islands moved, mountains removed) and parallels Isa 40:4 (mountains made low), Jer 4:24 (the mountains quaking), and Nah 1:5 (mountains quaking, hills melting). In the ancient world, islands and mountains were the most stable, immovable features of the earth -- their disappearance signals the total restructuring of the cosmic order. This is not geological destruction but theological: the stable things of the old world-order are dissolved in preparation for the new creation (21:1 -- "the first earth had passed away").
21: Hailstones "each weighing about a hundred pounds" echoes the seventh Egyptian plague (Exod 9:18-26 -- "hail and fire flashing continually in the midst of the hail, very heavy hail such as had never been in all the land of Egypt"). The measurement (about 100 pounds -- Greek talantiaion, a talent weight) signals total destructive force -- not hailstones that bruise but boulders that kill. The victims' response -- "they cursed God on account of the plague of hail" -- is the third and final refusal-to-repent in the bowl sequence, completing the Pharaoh-hardening pattern. The pattern of plague-curse-no-repentance is now complete.
